title: The Complete Guide to Plumbing Services in Chicago (2026)
author: Plumbers 911 Chicago
content:
Whether you've just bought your first Chicago bungalow or you've been maintaining a vintage Greystone for decades, plumbing is one of those systems you can't ignore. Chicago's unique combination of aging infrastructure, extreme weather swings, and strict building codes means plumbing here works differently than most other cities.

This comprehensive guide covers everything a Chicago homeowner needs to know about residential plumbing — from understanding your home's systems to choosing the right plumber, managing costs, and preventing the most common problems.

Table of Contents

  1. Understanding Your Chicago Home's Plumbing
  2. Types of Plumbing Services
  3. Plumbing Costs in Chicago
  4. Chicago Plumbing Codes & Permits
  5. Seasonal Plumbing Concerns
  6. How to Choose a Plumber
  7. Emergency Plumbing
  8. Preventive Maintenance

1. Understanding Your Chicago Home's Plumbing

Chicago's housing stock is diverse — from pre-1900 workers' cottages to modern high-rises — and each era brought different plumbing materials and practices.

Common Pipe Materials in Chicago Homes

MaterialEraLifespanCommon Issues
LeadPre-1940s100+ years (but toxic)Health hazard — Chicago is actively replacing these
Galvanized Steel1920s-1960s40-70 yearsCorrosion, reduced flow, rust-colored water
Cast Iron (drain)Pre-1970s75-100 yearsCorrosion, cracks, root intrusion at joints
Copper1960s-present50-70 yearsPinhole leaks from water chemistry, joint failures
PVC/ABS (drain)1970s-present50-100+ yearsJoint failures, not for hot water supply
PEX1990s-present40-50+ yearsFewer issues; flexible, freeze-resistant
Clay (sewer)Pre-1960s50-60 yearsRoot intrusion, cracking, bellying

If your Chicago home was built before 1960, there's a good chance you have galvanized supply pipes and clay sewer lines — both of which may be at or past their expected lifespan. Homes built between 1960-1990 likely have copper supply lines, which are still serviceable but may develop pinhole leaks. Modern homes (1990+) often use PEX supply lines and PVC drains.

Chicago's Water Supply

Chicago draws its water from Lake Michigan, one of the cleanest large-city water sources in the country. The city's water treatment facilities produce water that meets or exceeds all federal standards. However, the journey from the treatment plant to your faucet may introduce contaminants — particularly if your home or the city's service line is made of lead.

Water hardness: Chicago's water is moderately hard at approximately 8 grains per gallon (140 ppm). This isn't extreme, but it's enough to cause mineral buildup in water heaters, faucet aerators, and dishwashers over time. A water softener can help, but isn't strictly necessary for most Chicago homes.

Chicago's Sewer System

Chicago operates a combined sewer system, meaning both stormwater and wastewater flow through the same pipes. During heavy rainfall, this system can become overwhelmed, leading to basement backups — a common and costly problem for Chicago homeowners. The Metropolitan Water Reclamation District (MWRD) manages the system, and the city has invested billions in the Tunnel and Reservoir Plan (TARP, or "Deep Tunnel") to reduce flooding, but backups still occur.

2. Types of Plumbing Services

Plumbing services fall into several categories. Here's what each involves:

Emergency Plumbing

Available 24/7, emergency plumbing covers situations that can't wait: burst pipes, sewer backups, gas leaks, and complete loss of water. In Chicago, the most common winter emergency is frozen and burst pipes, while summer storms drive sewer backup calls. Emergency service typically costs more than scheduled visits, but the speed of response prevents far greater damage.

Drain & Sewer Services

The most frequently requested plumbing services in Chicago:

  • Drain Cleaning — Clearing clogs using cables, hydro-jetting, or enzymatic treatments
  • Sewer Camera Inspection — HD video to diagnose sewer line problems without excavation
  • Sewer Cleaning — Clearing main sewer line blockages
  • Sewer Replacement — Full line replacement when repair isn't viable
  • Drain Replacement — Replacing deteriorated interior drain lines

Water Heater Services

Water heaters are the second-most expensive appliance in most homes to operate. Chicago homeowners choose between:

  • Tank Water Heater Repair — Fixing thermostat, element, valve, or anode rod issues
  • Tank Water Heater Installation — Replacing units typically every 10-12 years
  • Tankless Water Heater Repair — Descaling, igniter, and component repairs
  • Tankless Water Heater Installation — Upgrading to on-demand hot water

Bathroom & Kitchen Plumbing

  • Bathroom Remodeling — Fixture installation, pipe rerouting, rough-in work
  • Kitchen Remodeling — Sink relocation, dishwasher, garbage disposal, pot fillers
  • Faucet Repair & Replacement
  • Toilet Installation

Water Line Services

  • Whole House Repiping — Replacing all supply pipes (common in pre-1960 homes)
  • Frozen & Broken Pipe Repair
  • Backflow Testing & Installation
  • Water Leak Detection & Repair

3. Plumbing Costs in Chicago (2026)

Chicago plumbing costs are influenced by the high cost of living, strict code requirements, and the complexity of working with aging infrastructure. Here are typical ranges:

ServiceTypical Cost Range
Emergency service call$150 - $300
Drain cleaning$150 - $500
Sewer camera inspection$200 - $500

These ranges reflect plumbing labor and materials only. Permits, surface restoration, and contractor coordination (for remodels) are additional. Always get at least 2-3 written estimates from licensed plumbers before committing to major work.

4. Chicago Plumbing Codes & Permits

Chicago has its own plumbing code, separate from the state of Illinois. Key things homeowners should know:

  • Licensed plumbers required — All plumbing work (beyond simple fixture swaps) must be performed by or supervised by a licensed plumber
  • Permits required for: water heater installation, sewer work, gas lines, new fixture installations, remodeling, and any work that modifies the plumbing system
  • Inspections are required after permitted work before walls are closed
  • Backflow prevention testing is required annually for commercial properties
  • Lead service line replacement is being managed citywide — check chicagowaterquality.org for your address

A reputable plumber handles all permitting and inspections as part of their service. If a plumber suggests skipping the permit, that's a red flag. Read our detailed Chicago plumbing code guide.

5. Seasonal Plumbing Concerns in Chicago

Winter (December - March)

  • Frozen pipes — Chicago's #1 winter plumbing problem. Insulate exposed pipes, let faucets drip during extreme cold
  • Water heater strain — Incoming water drops to 37-42°F, making heaters work harder
  • Sump pump ice — Discharge lines can freeze, causing pump failure

Read our complete frozen pipe prevention guide.

Spring (April - June)

  • Sump pump testing — Test before spring rains hit
  • Sewer backups — Heavy spring rains overwhelm combined sewers
  • Outdoor faucet restoration — Reconnect hoses, check for winter damage

Summer (July - September)

  • Sewer line stress — Tree roots are most active, seeking water
  • Garbage disposal overload — Cookout season increases disposal usage
  • Sprinkler system issues — Backflow prevention required

Fall (October - November)

  • Winterization — Disconnect hoses, insulate pipes, service water heater
  • Drain maintenance — Clear drains before heavy use over holidays
  • Water heater flushing — Annual maintenance before winter demand increases

6. How to Choose a Plumber in Chicago

Not all plumbers are created equal. Here's what to look for:

  1. Valid Illinois plumbing license — Verify at idfpr.illinois.gov
  2. Insurance — Liability insurance and workers' compensation are essential
  3. Google reviews — Look for 4+ stars with 50+ reviews
  4. Transparent pricing — Written estimates before work begins, no "discovery" fees
  5. 24/7 availability — Emergencies don't wait for business hours
  6. Warranty — Reputable plumbers stand behind their work
  7. Permits — They should handle all permitting without you asking

Red flags: Unusually low quotes (often leads to upselling), no license, cash-only, no written estimate, pressure to decide immediately.

7. Emergency Plumbing in Chicago

If you have a plumbing emergency:

  1. Gas leak? Leave the building, call 911, then call Peoples Gas (866-556-6002)
  2. Water emergency? Shut off the main water valve, then call a plumber
  3. Sewer backup? Stop using all water fixtures, call a plumber

Plumbers 911 Chicago is available 24/7 for emergencies at 833-758-6911. We typically dispatch a licensed plumber within 30-60 minutes in the Chicago metro area.

8. Preventive Maintenance Checklist

The best plumbing repair is the one you never need. Here's an annual maintenance schedule:

  • Test sump pump (pour water in pit)
  • Run water in unused fixtures to prevent trap drying

title: How to Unclog a Drain Without Chemicals: 5 Methods That Work
author: Plumbers 911 Chicago
content:
Chemical drain cleaners are tempting when you're staring at a slow drain, but they can damage your pipes — especially older cast iron and galvanized steel common in Chicago homes. Here are 5 safer methods that actually work.

Method 1: Boiling Water

Best for: Grease and soap buildup in kitchen sinks

Boil a full kettle of water. Pour it directly into the drain in 2-3 stages, waiting a few seconds between each pour. The heat melts grease and dissolves soap scum. Important: Only use on metal pipes. Boiling water can soften PVC pipe joints.

Method 2: Baking Soda + Vinegar

Best for: Moderate clogs in bathroom sinks and tubs

  1. Pour 1/2 cup baking soda down the drain
  2. Follow with 1/2 cup white vinegar
  3. Cover the drain opening immediately (the fizzing action needs to push downward)
  4. Wait 30 minutes
  5. Flush with hot (not boiling) water

The chemical reaction breaks down organic material. Repeat if needed. This is gentle enough for any pipe material.

Method 3: Plunger (The Right Way)

Best for: Solid blockages in sinks and toilets

Most people plunge wrong. For sinks, use a cup plunger (flat bottom). For toilets, use a flange plunger (extended rubber lip). Fill the sink/tub with enough water to cover the plunger cup, then plunge vigorously 15-20 times. The water pressure, not air, does the work.

Method 4: Drain Snake (Manual Auger)

Best for: Hair clogs and deeper blockages

A 25-foot drain snake ($15-$30 at any hardware store) reaches clogs that plungers can't. Insert the cable, turn the handle to push through the clog, then pull it out. This physically removes the obstruction rather than just pushing it further down.

Method 5: Wet/Dry Vacuum

Best for: Stubborn sink clogs when you have a shop-vac

Set your wet/dry vacuum to liquid mode. Create a tight seal over the drain (an old plunger cup works well). The vacuum's suction can pull the clog out of the drain.

When to Call a Professional

If none of these methods work, or if multiple drains are slow simultaneously, you likely have a deeper problem — possibly in your main sewer line. Call Plumbers 911 Chicago at 833-758-6911 for professional drain cleaning service.

title: How to Fix a Running Toilet in 10 Minutes
author: Plumbers 911 Chicago
content:
A running toilet isn't just annoying — it wastes up to 200 gallons of water per day, which can add $50+ to your monthly water bill. The good news: most running toilets can be fixed in 10 minutes without tools.

Step 1: Identify the Problem

Remove the tank lid and look inside while the toilet runs. The problem is almost always one of three things:

Problem A: The Flapper Is Worn

The flapper is the rubber seal at the bottom of the tank. If it's warped, cracked, or coated in mineral buildup, water leaks from the tank into the bowl continuously.

Fix: Turn off the water supply (valve behind the toilet), flush to empty the tank, unhook the old flapper, and snap on a new one ($3-$8 at any hardware store). Turn water back on.

Problem B: The Float Is Set Too High

If the water level in the tank is above the overflow tube, water constantly drains into the overflow.

Fix: Adjust the float. For a ball float, bend the arm slightly downward. For a cup float, pinch the clip and slide it down the rod. The water level should be about 1 inch below the overflow tube.

Problem C: The Fill Valve Is Faulty

If adjusting the float doesn't help and the flapper is fine, the fill valve itself may be stuck open.

Fix: Try cleaning the fill valve by removing the cap and rinsing debris. If that doesn't work, replace the entire fill valve ($10-$15, 15-minute job).

When to Call a Plumber

If the toilet continues running after replacing the flapper and adjusting the float, or if you notice cracks in the porcelain or water on the floor, call 833-758-6911 for professional toilet service.

title: How to Shut Off Your Water in an Emergency (Chicago Home Guide)
author: Plumbers 911 Chicago
content:
In a plumbing emergency, the single most important thing you can do is shut off the water supply. A burst pipe can release 250+ gallons per hour. Every minute of flooding adds to the damage. Knowing where your shutoff valve is — and testing it before an emergency — can save you thousands.

Finding Your Main Shutoff Valve

Chicago Homes (Most Common Locations)

  • Basement, front wall — The most common location in Chicago bungalows, two-flats, and Greystones. Look where the water line enters the house from the street
  • Utility closet or mechanical room — In condos and newer construction
  • Near the water meter — The shutoff is usually within a few feet of the meter
  • Crawl space — Some older homes have the valve in the crawl space near the front foundation wall

Types of Shutoff Valves

  • Gate valve (round handle) — Turn clockwise to close. May require several full turns. Common in older Chicago homes
  • Ball valve (lever handle) — Quarter turn to close (perpendicular to pipe = off). Modern, more reliable

How to Shut Off Water

  1. Locate the main shutoff valve
  2. Turn clockwise (gate) or perpendicular to pipe (ball valve)
  3. Open a faucet to verify water stops flowing
  4. If the valve is stuck, don't force it — you could break the valve. Call a plumber

Shutting Off Individual Fixtures

For localized leaks, shut off just the affected fixture:

  • Toilet: Oval valve on the wall behind/below the tank
  • Sink: Valves under the sink (hot and cold)
  • Washing machine: Valves behind the machine on the wall
  • Water heater: Valve on the cold water inlet pipe on top

Test Your Valve Now

Don't wait for an emergency. Go find your main shutoff valve right now. Turn it off and on to make sure it works. If it's stuck or leaking, call 833-758-6911 to have it replaced before you need it.

Label it. Put a tag or sticker on the valve so anyone in your household can find it instantly during an emergency.

title: Spring Plumbing Checklist for Chicago Homeowners (2026)
author: Plumbers 911 Chicago
content:

Exterior Checklist

  • Inspect outdoor faucets — Turn each on and check for leaks or reduced flow (signs of freeze damage)
  • Reconnect garden hoses — Check hose bibs for drips when hose is connected
  • Check sewer cleanout — Ensure the cleanout cap is in place and accessible

  • Clear debris from floor drains — Basement window wells, stairwell drains

Interior Checklist

  • Test sump pump — Pour a bucket of water into the pit. The pump should activate and clear the water within seconds. If not, service immediately before spring rains
  • Check sump pump battery backup — Replace batteries if older than 2 years
  • Inspect exposed pipes — Look for any new leaks, corrosion, or frost damage from winter
  • Run all fixtures — Turn on every faucet, flush every toilet. Check for slow drains
  • Check water heater — Look for rust, leaks, or unusual sounds. Spring is ideal for annual flushing
  • Test water pressure — Should be 40-80 PSI. Low pressure may indicate a leak

Professional Service Recommendations

  • Sewer camera inspection — If you experienced any backups over winter, get a camera inspection to check for root intrusion or damage
  • Water heater flush — Annual flushing removes sediment and extends unit life
  • Backflow preventer testing — Required annually for commercial properties in Chicago

title: Summer Plumbing Tips: Protect Your Chicago Home
author: Plumbers 911 Chicago
content:
Summer in Chicago means cookouts, house guests, and heavy plumbing usage. Here's how to keep your system running smoothly during the busiest months.

Kitchen Plumbing

  • Garbage disposal care — Never put corn husks, watermelon rinds, bones, or grease down the disposal. Run cold water for 15 seconds before and after using it
  • Grease disposal — Pour cooking grease into a container and throw it away. Never down the drain. Grease solidifies in pipes and causes major clogs
  • Dishwasher loading — Scrape plates before loading. Food particles accumulate in drain lines over time

Bathroom Plumbing

  • Guest bathroom prep — Run water in guest bathrooms to refill P-traps (prevents sewer gas odors). Check for any slow drains before company arrives
  • Toilet awareness — Only flush toilet paper and waste. Remind guests: no wipes, feminine products, or cotton swabs

Outdoor Plumbing

  • Sprinkler systems — Check for leaks and broken heads. Ensure backflow preventer is tested
  • Hose bibs — Check for leaks at connections. A dripping hose bib wastes water and can damage siding or foundation
  • Pool plumbing — If you have a pool, check pump connections and plumbing for leaks

Sewer Line Awareness

Summer is when tree roots are most aggressive. They seek moisture and can infiltrate sewer line cracks and joints. Watch for:

  • Slow drains throughout the house
  • Gurgling sounds from drains
  • Patches of extra-green grass over sewer line
  • Sewage odors outdoors

If you notice any of these signs, call 833-758-6911 for a sewer camera inspection before the problem gets worse.

title: Fall Plumbing Winterization Guide for Illinois Homeowners
author: Plumbers 911 Chicago
content:
In Illinois, the first hard freeze can come as early as late October. Winterizing your plumbing before temperatures drop below 32°F prevents frozen pipes, burst water lines, and thousands of dollars in water damage.

Outdoor Winterization (Do Before First Freeze)

  1. Disconnect all garden hoses — Water left in hoses can freeze back into the faucet and split the pipe inside your wall
  2. Shut off outdoor faucet supply — Turn off the indoor shutoff valve for each outdoor faucet, then open the outdoor faucet to drain remaining water
  3. Drain sprinkler systems — Use compressed air to blow out remaining water from irrigation lines
  4. Insulate outdoor pipes — Any exposed pipes in garages, crawl spaces, or along exterior walls need foam insulation

Indoor Winterization

  1. Insulate exposed pipes — Basement pipes, pipes in exterior walls, and pipes near windows are most vulnerable
  2. Service your water heater — Flush the tank and check the anode rod. Your heater works hardest in winter
  3. Test sump pump — Ensure it's working before ground freezes. Consider a battery backup if you don't have one
  4. Check sump pump discharge line — Make sure it drains away from the house and won't freeze and back up
  5. Locate your main shutoff valve — Make sure you know where it is and that it works. Label it
  6. Set thermostat — Never below 55°F, even when away. Open cabinet doors under sinks on exterior walls during extreme cold

For Vacation Homes / Snowbirds

If you're leaving your Illinois property unoccupied for winter:

  • Set heat to at least 55°F
  • Consider shutting off the main water supply and draining the system
  • Have someone check the property weekly
  • Or hire a plumber for a full winterization service

What Is a Plumbing Rough-In?

title: Pipe Repair vs Replacement: When to Choose Which
author: Plumbers 911 Chicago
content:
When a plumber tells you there's a pipe problem, the first question is always: repair or replace? The answer depends on several factors.

Choose Repair When:

  • Damage is isolated to one spot (single leak, one joint failure)
  • Pipes are less than 30 years old and in otherwise good condition
  • The pipe material is copper or PEX (long-lasting materials)
  • The leak is accessible (not buried under concrete or behind load-bearing walls)
  • Cost of repair is less than 30% of replacement cost

Typical repair cost: $150 - $800

Choose Replacement When:

  • Pipes are galvanized steel (corrodes from inside, one leak means more are coming)
  • Multiple leaks in different locations (systemic failure, not isolated)
  • Pipes are 50+ years old regardless of current condition
  • Water is discolored (rust from inside corroded pipes)
  • Water pressure is consistently low throughout the house
  • You're doing a major remodel anyway (access to pipes is already open)

Typical replacement cost: $2,000 - $15,000+ (partial to whole-house)

The 50% Rule

A good rule of thumb: if the repair cost exceeds 50% of the replacement cost, replace. You'll spend less in the long run and get the peace of mind of new plumbing.

Not sure which you need? Call 833-758-6911 for an honest assessment. We'll recommend repair when repair makes sense, and replacement when it doesn't.

title: Why Is My Water Bill So High? 7 Hidden Leaks to Check
author: Plumbers 911 Chicago
content:
If your Chicago water bill suddenly spiked without a change in usage, you likely have a hidden leak. Even small leaks waste hundreds of gallons per month. Here's where to look.

1. Running Toilet

The #1 cause of high water bills. A running toilet can waste 200 gallons per day. Put a few drops of food coloring in the tank. If color appears in the bowl within 30 minutes without flushing, you have a leak. Fix it yourself or call us.

2. Dripping Faucets

A faucet dripping once per second wastes 3,000 gallons per year. Check all faucets in your home, including bathtub spouts and outdoor hose bibs.

3. Leaking Water Heater

Check around the base of your water heater and along the pressure relief valve discharge pipe. Small drips can waste significant water and indicate a failing tank.

4. Underground Supply Line Leak

If your water meter continues spinning when all fixtures are off, you may have an underground leak between the street and your house. Look for wet spots in your yard even during dry weather.

5. Toilet Flapper Degradation

Different from a running toilet — a worn flapper can create a slow, silent leak that wastes water without the obvious running sound. The food coloring test catches this.

6. Irrigation System Leaks

Broken sprinkler heads, cracked lines, and faulty valves can waste enormous amounts of water underground where you can't see it.

7. Slab Leak

Hot water pipes running under your concrete slab can develop pinhole leaks. Signs include warm spots on floors, the sound of running water when nothing is on, and unexplained moisture.

How to Test for a Leak

  1. Turn off all water fixtures in your home
  2. Check your water meter
  3. Wait 2 hours without using any water
  4. Check the meter again — if it moved, you have a leak

Can't find the leak? Call 833-758-6911 for professional water leak detection. We use acoustic and thermal technology to pinpoint hidden leaks without tearing up your home.

title: Low Water Pressure in Chicago? 8 Causes and How to Fix Them
author: Plumbers 911 Chicago
content:
Low water pressure makes everything harder — showering, washing dishes, even filling a glass takes forever. In Chicago, there are several common causes, many of which are fixable.

1. Clogged Faucet Aerator

Symptom: Low pressure at one faucet only

Fix: Unscrew the aerator at the tip of the faucet. Clean the screen of mineral buildup. Soak in vinegar overnight for stubborn deposits. Screw it back on.

2. Corroded Galvanized Pipes

Symptom: Low pressure throughout the house, gradually worsening over years

Fix: Many older Chicago homes have galvanized steel pipes that corrode internally, narrowing the flow path. The only permanent fix is repiping with copper or PEX.

3. Partially Closed Shutoff Valve

Symptom: Low pressure throughout the house, started suddenly

Fix: Check your main shutoff valve and make sure it's fully open. Gate valves need to be turned completely counterclockwise. Ball valves should be parallel to the pipe.

4. Pressure Regulator Failure

Symptom: Sudden change in pressure throughout house

Fix: Some homes have a pressure-reducing valve where the main line enters. These can fail after 10-15 years. Replacement costs $200-$400.

5. City Water Main Issue

Symptom: Low pressure affecting your neighbors too

Fix: Contact the City of Chicago Department of Water Management at 312-744-4426. This is their responsibility to fix.

6. Water Heater Issues (Hot Water Only)

Symptom: Low pressure only on hot water side

Fix: Sediment buildup in the water heater tank restricts flow. A professional water heater flush usually restores pressure.

7. Hidden Leak

Symptom: Gradual pressure decrease, possibly with higher water bill

Fix: A leak anywhere in your system reduces pressure. Professional leak detection pinpoints the problem.

8. Peak Usage Times

Symptom: Low pressure during morning/evening only

Fix: This is normal during peak usage. If it's severe, a pressure booster pump ($500-$1,000 installed) can help.

Need help with water pressure? Call 833-758-6911. We diagnose the cause and provide the right solution — not just a band-aid.

title: Chicago's Lead Pipe Replacement Program: What Homeowners Need to Know
author: Plumbers 911 Chicago
content:
Chicago has more lead water service lines than any other American city — an estimated 400,000+ homes are connected to the water main via lead pipes installed before 1986. The city has launched an ambitious replacement program, and here's what every homeowner needs to know.

What Is a Lead Service Line?

A lead service line is the pipe that connects the city's water main in the street to your home's internal plumbing. In Chicago, these are typically 3/4-inch to 1-inch diameter lead pipes installed when the home was built. While the city's water treatment adds phosphate to create a protective coating inside these pipes, the coating can be disrupted by construction, temperature changes, or water chemistry fluctuations.

How to Check If Your Home Has Lead Pipes

  1. Check online: Visit chicagowaterquality.org and enter your address
  2. Physical test: Find where the water line enters your home (usually in the basement). Scratch the pipe gently with a coin. Lead is soft and will show a shiny silver color. Copper shows a penny-like color. Galvanized steel is dull gray and magnetic
  3. Check your home's age: If built before 1986 in Chicago, it almost certainly has a lead service line

The City's Replacement Program

Chicago has allocated significant funding for lead service line replacement. Key details:

  • The city replaces the public portion (from the water main to the property line)
  • Homeowners are responsible for the private portion (from the property line into the house)
  • The city offers financial assistance programs for qualifying homeowners
  • Replacement is coordinated with other infrastructure work when possible to reduce costs

What Homeowners Pay

The private-side replacement (property line to house) typically costs $3,000 - $7,000 depending on length and complexity. Some cost-sharing programs are available. Contact 311 or visit the city's website for current program details.

Interim Protection

If your home has lead pipes and replacement hasn't happened yet:

  • Run cold water for 1-2 minutes before drinking (especially first thing in the morning)
  • Only use cold water for cooking and drinking (hot water dissolves more lead)
  • Consider a water filtration system certified for lead removal (NSF/ANSI Standard 53)
  • Get a free lead test kit from the city at chicagowaterquality.org

Need help with lead pipe assessment or water filtration? Call 833-758-6911. We can inspect your service line, install lead-certified water filtration, and coordinate with the city's replacement program.

title: Chicago Basement Flooding: Causes, Prevention & What to Do
author: Plumbers 911 Chicago
content:
Basement flooding is one of the most common — and most expensive — problems Chicago homeowners face. The city's combined sewer system, high water table, and intense storms create a perfect recipe for water in your basement. Here's what causes it and how to prevent it.

Why Chicago Basements Flood

1. Combined Sewer Overflow (CSO)

Chicago's sewer system carries both sewage and stormwater in the same pipes. During heavy rain, the system can't handle the volume, and water backs up through floor drains and toilets in basements. This is the most common cause of basement flooding in Chicago and unfortunately one of the hardest to prevent completely.

2. Failed Sump Pump

If your sump pump fails during a storm — due to mechanical failure, power outage, or an overwhelmed pump — groundwater floods your basement. This is preventable with proper maintenance and a battery backup system.

3. Sewer Line Backup

Tree roots, pipe collapse, or blockages in your private sewer line can cause sewage to back up into your basement. This requires immediate professional attention and is a health hazard.

4. Foundation Cracks

Water seeping through cracks in your basement walls or floor is common in older Chicago homes. This is often groundwater pressure (hydrostatic pressure) forcing water through any available path.

5. Poor Grading/Drainage

If the ground around your foundation slopes toward the house instead of away, rainwater pools at the foundation and finds its way in.

Prevention Strategies

Essential (Every Chicago Home Should Have)

  • Working sump pump — Test monthly by pouring water into the pit
  • Battery backup sump pump — A $500-$1,500 investment that prevents thousands in flood damage. Learn about battery backup installation
  • Backwater valve (overhead sewer) — Prevents city sewer from backing up into your home. Required by Chicago code on some properties
  • Clear floor drains — Keep basement floor drains clear of debris

Recommended

  • Downspout extensions — Route roof water at least 6 feet from foundation
  • Window well covers — Keep rain out of basement window wells
  • Proper grading — Ground should slope away from house at 1 inch per foot for the first 6 feet
  • Sewer camera inspection — Annual inspection catches root intrusion and damage early

What to Do When Your Basement Floods

  1. Safety first: Don't enter standing water if there's any chance of electrical exposure. Turn off power to the basement if you can safely reach the breaker
  2. Stop the source: If it's a plumbing failure, shut off the water. If it's sewer backup, stop using all water fixtures
  3. Call for help: 833-758-6911 for 24/7 emergency plumbing service
  4. Document everything: Photos and video before cleanup begins (for insurance)
  5. Don't pump too fast: Removing water too quickly can cause foundation walls to cave in from external soil pressure
  6. Disinfect after sewer backup: Sewage contains dangerous bacteria. Professional cleanup is recommended

Chicago Resources

  • Report flooding: Call 311 or use the CHI 311 app
  • MWRD stormwater rebate: Up to $2,000 for approved flood mitigation. Visit mwrd.org
  • City of Chicago basement flooding assistance: Contact your alderman's office

Need sump pump service, backwater valve installation, or emergency flood help? Call Plumbers 911 Chicago at 833-758-6911. Available 24/7.

title: Sump Pump Buying Guide for Chicago Homes (2026)
author: Plumbers 911 Chicago
content:
If you live in the Chicago area, a sump pump isn't optional — it's essential. Chicago's high water table, combined sewer system, and intense spring/summer storms mean basements flood regularly. Here's how to choose the right sump pump.

Submersible vs Pedestal Sump Pumps

FeatureSubmersiblePedestal
LocationInside the sump pit, underwaterMotor sits above the pit
Noise levelQuieter (water muffles sound)Louder
Lifespan5-15 years15-25 years
Cost$100-$400$60-$200
Power1/3 to 1 HP (handles more water)1/3 to 1/2 HP
Best forMost Chicago homes (recommended)Shallow pits, tight budgets

How Much Horsepower Do You Need?

  • 1/3 HP — Standard for most homes with normal water table. Pumps ~2,500 gallons/hour
  • 1/2 HP — Recommended for Chicago homes with higher water tables or history of flooding. Pumps ~3,500 gallons/hour
  • 3/4 to 1 HP — For homes with serious water issues, deep basements, or long discharge lines

Battery Backup: Non-Negotiable in Chicago

Power outages and storms often coincide in Chicago. A battery backup sump pump runs when the power goes out — exactly when you need it most. Budget $300-$600 for a quality battery backup system. Learn about battery backup installation.

Top Sump Pump Brands

  • Zoeller — Industry standard, excellent reliability, made in the USA
  • Wayne — Good value, wide product range
  • Liberty Pumps — Premium quality, great customer support
  • Superior Pump — Budget-friendly, solid performance

Sump Pump Maintenance

Test your sump pump monthly by pouring water into the pit until the float triggers. Listen for unusual sounds. Replace the battery backup every 2-3 years. Consider professional inspection annually — call 833-758-6911 for sump pump service.

title: How to Read Your Water Meter to Detect Leaks
author: Plumbers 911 Chicago
content:
Your water meter is the most accurate tool for detecting hidden leaks in your home. If the meter moves when nothing is running, you have a leak somewhere. Here's how to use it.

Finding Your Water Meter in Chicago

In most Chicago homes, the water meter is in the basement, near where the water main enters the house (typically the front wall). It's a round dial or digital display connected to the main water line. In some newer homes or condos, it may be in a utility closet.

How to Read the Meter

Analog Meters (Most Common in Chicago)

  • The large sweep hand measures 1 gallon per revolution
  • The odometer-style numbers show total gallons used
  • The small triangle or star near the center is the flow indicator — if it's spinning, water is flowing somewhere in your home

Digital Meters

  • Shine a flashlight on the display to activate it
  • It shows current reading in gallons
  • Some show flow rate in real-time

The Leak Detection Test

  1. Turn off every water fixture in your home — faucets, toilets, dishwasher, washing machine, ice maker, sprinklers
  2. Check the flow indicator (small triangle). If it's moving, you have a leak
  3. Write down the meter reading
  4. Wait 2 hours without using any water
  5. Read the meter again. If the number changed, you have a leak

Estimating Leak Size

If your meter moved by 10 gallons in 2 hours, you're losing 120 gallons per day — that's a significant leak (likely a running toilet). A 1-2 gallon change suggests a slow drip.

title: Garbage Disposal Do's and Don'ts: Avoid Costly Repairs
author: Plumbers 911 Chicago
content:
Your garbage disposal is one of the hardest-working appliances in your kitchen. Treat it right and it'll last 10-15 years. Abuse it and you're looking at a clog, jam, or expensive replacement.

DO's

  • DO run cold water before, during, and 15 seconds after using the disposal. Cold water solidifies grease so it gets chopped up rather than coating pipes
  • DO feed small amounts gradually. Don't dump a full plate at once
  • DO grind citrus peels occasionally to clean and deodorize
  • DO run it regularly, even if you have nothing to grind. Prevents rust and corrosion
  • DO use ice cubes periodically to clean the grinding chamber

DON'Ts

  • DON'T put grease, oil, or fat down the disposal. It coats blades and clogs pipes
  • DON'T put fibrous foods: celery, corn husks, asparagus, artichokes. Fibers wrap around blades
  • DON'T put starchy foods: pasta, rice, potato peels. They expand and create paste-like clogs
  • DON'T put bones, fruit pits, or shells. They damage blades
  • DON'T put coffee grounds. They accumulate in pipes like sediment
  • DON'T use hot water while grinding. It liquefies grease which then re-solidifies in pipes
  • DON'T put your hand in. Ever. Use tongs or pliers to retrieve dropped items
  • DON'T use chemical drain cleaners. They corrode disposal components

Quick Fix: Disposal Won't Turn On

  1. Make sure it's plugged in
  2. Press the red reset button on the bottom of the unit
  3. If it hums but doesn't spin, it's jammed — turn it off and use the hex wrench (included with most disposals) in the bottom socket to manually free the jam

Need disposal repair or replacement? Call 833-758-6911 for garbage disposal service.

title: What Causes Sewer Gas Smell in Your House? 6 Common Sources
author: Plumbers 911 Chicago
content:
A sewer gas smell in your home isn't just unpleasant — it can indicate a plumbing problem and even pose health risks. Here are the most common causes and what to do about each one.

1. Dry P-Trap

Most common cause. Every drain has a P-shaped trap that holds water to block sewer gases. If a drain hasn't been used in weeks, the water evaporates and gas comes through.

Fix: Run water in every drain for 30 seconds, especially guest bathrooms, basement drains, and laundry drains you don't use often. Do this monthly.

2. Failed Toilet Wax Ring

The wax ring between your toilet and the floor flange creates an airtight seal. When it fails, sewer gas seeps around the toilet base.

Fix: If you smell sewer near a toilet (especially if it rocks when you sit), the wax ring needs replacement ($5 part, 30-minute job for a plumber).

3. Blocked Plumbing Vent

Your plumbing system has vent pipes that exit through the roof, allowing air into the system and releasing sewer gases safely above your home. If a vent is blocked (leaves, bird nests, ice in winter), gases back up into the house.

Fix: Listen for gurgling drains — that's a classic sign of a blocked vent. This requires a plumber to clear the vent from the roof.

4. Cracked Sewer Line

A cracked or broken sewer line under or near your home can release sewer gas into the soil, which seeps through foundation cracks into your basement.

Fix: If the smell is strongest in your basement and you can't find another source, get a sewer camera inspection.

5. Loose Sewer Cleanout Cap

The sewer cleanout (usually a white PVC cap in the basement floor or outside) can loosen over time, releasing gas.

Fix: Check that all cleanout caps are tight and properly sealed.

6. Bacteria in Drain

Organic buildup inside drains creates a biofilm that produces hydrogen sulfide (rotten egg smell). Common in kitchen sinks and shower drains.

Fix: Pour baking soda and vinegar down the drain, wait 30 minutes, flush with hot water. For persistent odor, professional drain cleaning eliminates the biofilm.

title: How to Prevent Basement Flooding in Chicago: Complete Guide
author: Plumbers 911 Chicago
content:
Chicago ranks among the worst cities for basement flooding. The combined sewer system, flat terrain, clay soil, and intense storms create the perfect conditions for water in your basement. Here's everything you can do to prevent it.

Essential Prevention (Every Chicago Home)

  1. Install a quality sump pump — 1/2 HP submersible minimum for Chicago. See our buying guide
  2. Add battery backup — Power outages during storms are common. A battery backup keeps your pump running. Battery backup installation
  3. Install a backwater valve — Prevents city sewer from backing up into your home through floor drains. Chicago code requires them on some properties
  4. Test sump pump monthly — Pour water into the pit until the pump activates. Listen for unusual sounds
  5. Keep floor drains clear — Remove debris from basement floor drain covers

Exterior Prevention

  1. Grade soil away from foundation — Ground should slope 1 inch per foot for the first 6 feet from the house
  2. Extend downspouts — Discharge rain at least 6 feet from the foundation. Add underground extensions if possible
  3. Install window well covers — Prevents rain from filling window wells and seeping in
  4. Seal foundation cracks — Hydraulic cement or epoxy injection for active leaks
  5. Clean gutters — Clogged gutters overflow directly at the foundation

Advanced Prevention

  • Interior drain tile system — French drain around the interior basement perimeter, directing water to the sump pit ($5,000-$15,000)
  • Exterior waterproofing — Excavating around foundation and applying waterproof membrane ($10,000-$25,000)
  • Overhead sewer conversion — Raises your sewer connection above the basement floor, eliminating backup risk ($3,000-$8,000)

Chicago Financial Help

  • MWRD stormwater rebate: Up to $2,000 for approved flood mitigation projects. Visit mwrd.org
  • City of Chicago basement flooding program: Contact your alderman's office or 311

title: Water Softener vs Water Filter: Which Does Your Chicago Home Need?
author: Plumbers 911 Chicago
content:
These two systems are often confused, but they solve completely different problems. Here's how to decide which your Chicago home needs — or if you need both.

What Each System Does

Water SoftenerWater Filter
RemovesCalcium, magnesium (hardness minerals)Chlorine, lead, sediment, contaminants
PurposePrevents scale buildup in pipes/appliancesImproves taste and safety of drinking water
How it worksIon exchange (replaces minerals with sodium)Physical/chemical filtration (carbon, RO, etc.)
Cost$1,000-$3,000 installed$200-$2,000+ installed
MaintenanceAdd salt monthly ($5-$10/bag)Replace filters every 3-12 months

What Does Chicago Water Need?

Water hardness: Chicago's water is moderately hard at ~8 grains per gallon. A softener is helpful but not critical. If you notice white scale on faucets or your water heater runs less efficiently over time, a softener helps.

Lead concern: If your home has lead service lines (common in pre-1986 Chicago homes), a water filter certified for lead removal (NSF Standard 53) is strongly recommended. Learn about Chicago's lead pipes.

Taste: If you notice a chlorine taste, a carbon filter improves it dramatically.

Our Recommendation for Chicago

  • Pre-1986 home with lead pipes: Water filter (lead removal) is a priority. Softener optional
  • Scale buildup problems: Water softener first
  • Better drinking water taste: Under-sink or whole-house carbon filter
  • Maximum protection: Both systems (softener for the whole house, filter for drinking water)

title: How to Choose the Right Toilet for Your Bathroom
author: Plumbers 911 Chicago
content:
Toilets come in more varieties than most people realize. Choosing the right one affects comfort, water bills, maintenance, and bathroom aesthetics. Here's what to consider.

Bowl Shape: Round vs Elongated

  • Round: 16.5 inches long. Fits in tight spaces. Lower cost. Common in half-baths
  • Elongated: 18.5 inches long. More comfortable. Recommended for full bathrooms. Most popular choice

Height: Standard vs Comfort

  • Standard: 15 inches to seat. Traditional height
  • Comfort/ADA: 17-19 inches to seat. Easier for adults, elderly, and anyone with mobility issues. Increasingly the default choice

Flush Types

  • Gravity flush: Most common. Simple, reliable, quiet. Uses water weight to create flush pressure
  • Pressure-assist: Uses compressed air for a powerful flush. Louder but less likely to clog. Good for families
  • Dual flush: Two buttons — 0.8 GPF for liquid, 1.6 GPF for solid. Saves water over time

Water Efficiency

Federal law requires toilets to use 1.6 gallons per flush (GPF) or less. WaterSense-labeled toilets use 1.28 GPF or less. For a family of 4 flushing ~20 times daily, upgrading from an old 3.5 GPF toilet to a 1.28 GPF model saves 16,000+ gallons annually.

How to Measure for Replacement

The critical measurement is the rough-in distance — the distance from the wall (not the baseboard) to the center of the drain bolts. Standard is 12 inches. Older Chicago homes sometimes have 10-inch or 14-inch rough-ins.

Installation Cost

Toilet installation in Chicago typically costs $200-$500 including labor and removal of the old unit. The toilet itself ranges from $100 (basic) to $800+ (premium/smart toilet).

title: Chicago Water Quality Report: What's Actually in Your Tap Water?
author: Plumbers 911 Chicago
content:
Chicago's water comes from Lake Michigan and is treated at two of the largest water treatment plants in the world. The treated water generally meets or exceeds all federal safety standards. But what happens between the plant and your faucet matters too.

Water Source & Treatment

Lake Michigan water is treated with chlorine (disinfection), fluoride (dental health), and blended phosphate (to coat lead pipes and reduce lead leaching). The city tests hundreds of samples daily.

What's In Chicago's Water

SubstanceLevelNotes
Hardness~140 ppm (8 grains/gallon)Moderately hard. Causes scale buildup
Chlorine~0.8 ppmSafe but affects taste. Carbon filter removes it
Fluoride~0.7 ppmAdded for dental health. Within EPA guidelines
LeadVaries by homeDepends on your service line material. Test your home
pH~7.6Slightly alkaline. Normal

The Lead Issue

Chicago has an estimated 400,000+ lead service lines connecting homes to the water main. While the city adds phosphate to create a protective coating inside these pipes, disruptions (construction, temperature changes, water chemistry shifts) can cause lead to leach into your water. Read about the city's replacement program.

How to Improve Your Water Quality

  • Run cold water 1-2 minutes before drinking (flushes standing water from lead pipes)
  • Use only cold water for cooking and drinking
  • Install a water filter certified for lead removal (NSF Standard 53). Water filter installation
  • Consider a water softener if scale is an issue. Water softener installation
  • Get your water tested — Free lead test kits at chicagowaterquality.org

Questions about your water quality? Call 833-758-6911.

title: Plumbing Tips for First-Time Chicago Homebuyers
author: Plumbers 911 Chicago
content:
Buying your first home in Chicago is exciting — but plumbing problems are among the most expensive surprises new homeowners face. Here's what to check before you close and what to do after.

Before You Buy: Plumbing Inspection Checklist

A general home inspection covers plumbing basics, but consider a dedicated plumbing inspection for older Chicago homes. Ask your inspector to check:

  • Pipe material — Lead, galvanized steel, cast iron, copper, or PEX? Lead and galvanized may need replacement
  • Water heater age — Check the manufacture date on the label. Replace if 10+ years old
  • Water pressure — Should be 40-80 PSI. Low pressure suggests pipe corrosion
  • Sewer line condition — Request a sewer camera inspection ($200-$500). This can reveal thousands in hidden problems
  • Sump pump presence and condition — Essential for Chicago basements
  • Lead service line — Check chicagowaterquality.org for your address

Red Flags That Should Concern You

  • Galvanized steel supply pipes (expect repiping in near future: $4,000-$15,000)
  • Clay sewer line (replacement may be needed: $3,000-$25,000)
  • No sump pump in a home with a basement
  • Water stains on basement walls or floors
  • Unusual patches in the yard above the sewer line
  • Low water pressure throughout the home

After You Close: First 30 Days

  1. Locate the main water shutoff valve and test it. Read our shutoff guide
  2. Locate the sewer cleanout
  3. Test all fixtures — Run every faucet, flush every toilet
  4. Test the sump pump
  5. Change the water heater temperature to 120°F
  6. Save a plumber's number — You'll need one eventually. 833-758-6911

title: Water Heater Anode Rod: What It Is and Why It Matters
author: Plumbers 911 Chicago
content:
Inside every tank water heater is a metal rod you've probably never thought about — the anode rod. It's the single most important factor in how long your water heater lasts.

What Is an Anode Rod?

An anode rod is a metal rod (usually magnesium or aluminum) suspended inside the water heater tank. It attracts corrosive elements in the water, sacrificing itself so the tank doesn't corrode. That's why it's called a "sacrificial anode" — it corrodes so the tank doesn't have to.

Why It Matters

When the anode rod is fully corroded (consumed), those corrosive elements start attacking the tank itself. Once the tank corrodes, it rusts through and leaks — and at that point, replacement is the only option. A new anode rod costs $20-$50. A new water heater costs $1,500-$3,500.

When to Replace It

  • Check every 3 years starting when the water heater is 3 years old
  • Replace when: the rod is less than 1/2 inch thick, coated in calcium, or more than 6 inches of core wire is exposed
  • Chicago water note: Chicago's moderately hard water causes anode rods to corrode faster. Check every 2-3 years

Signs Your Anode Rod Needs Replacement

  • Rotten egg smell in hot water (sulfur bacteria on a depleted rod)
  • Rust-colored hot water
  • Popping/crackling sounds from the tank (sediment buildup accelerated by depleted rod)

Can You Do It Yourself?

Technically yes — it's a single bolt on top of the heater. But you need a 1-1/16 inch socket, significant clearance above the tank (the rod is 3-4 feet long), and the ability to handle a potentially stuck fitting on a pressurized tank. Most homeowners prefer to have a plumber handle it during annual maintenance.

title: How to Winterize a Vacant Chicago Property
author: Plumbers 911 Chicago
content:
An unoccupied Chicago property during winter is at severe risk of frozen pipes, burst water lines, and flooding. Whether you're a snowbird, landlord between tenants, or managing an estate, proper winterization is essential.

Full Winterization (No Heat)

If you're shutting off heat completely:

  1. Shut off the main water supply
  2. Open all faucets (hot and cold) to drain remaining water
  3. Flush all toilets and hold the handle to drain the tank
  4. Pour RV antifreeze (non-toxic propylene glycol) into every drain trap: sinks, tubs, showers, floor drains, washing machine drain. Use 1-2 cups per drain
  5. Pour antifreeze into toilet bowls to prevent trap water from freezing
  6. Drain the water heater — Connect a hose to the drain valve and empty completely
  7. Drain the dishwasher and washing machine lines
  8. Shut off gas to the water heater
  9. Leave cabinet doors open under sinks on exterior walls
  10. Disconnect garden hoses and shut off exterior faucets from inside

Partial Winterization (Keeping Heat On)

If you're keeping heat on at a minimum temperature:

  • Set thermostat to minimum 55°F (the absolute minimum to prevent freezing)
  • Open cabinet doors under sinks on exterior walls
  • Set faucets on exterior walls to a slow drip during extreme cold
  • Test sump pump before leaving
  • Have someone check the property weekly

Spring De-Winterization

  1. Turn on the water supply slowly (watch for leaks)
  2. Close all faucets you opened
  3. Fill the water heater before turning on the gas/electric
  4. Run all fixtures and check for leaks
  5. Test sump pump

Need professional winterization or de-winterization? Call 833-758-6911. We handle residential and commercial property winterization across the Chicago metro.

title: Sewer Lining vs Sewer Replacement: Complete Comparison
author: Plumbers 911 Chicago
content:
When your sewer line fails, you have two main options: trenchless lining (cured-in-place pipe or CIPP) or traditional replacement. Here's how they compare.

Side-by-Side Comparison

FactorSewer Lining (CIPP)Traditional Replacement
Cost$4,000-$15,000$3,000-$25,000
Timeline1 day2-5 days
ExcavationMinimal (1-2 small access points)Full trench along pipe
Landscape damageMinimalSignificant (yard, driveway, sidewalk)
Lifespan50+ years50-100+ years
Pipe diameterSlightly reduced (liner thickness)Same or larger
Works forCracks, root damage, joint failuresCollapsed pipes, severe bellying, complete failure

Choose Lining When:

  • Pipe is structurally intact but has cracks, root intrusion, or joint failures
  • Pipe runs under a driveway, patio, or landscaping you want to preserve
  • You need the fastest possible repair
  • Budget allows for the typically higher per-foot cost

Choose Replacement When:

  • Pipe has collapsed or has severe bellying (sag)
  • Pipe diameter is too small (can upsize during replacement)
  • Multiple problems along the entire length
  • The line runs through easily accessible yard

Need a sewer diagnosis? A sewer camera inspection determines which method is right. Call 833-758-6911.

title: Submersible vs Pedestal Sump Pump: Which Is Better?
author: Plumbers 911 Chicago
content:
Choosing between submersible and pedestal sump pumps is the first decision when installing or replacing your pump. Here's a detailed comparison.

Comparison Table

FeatureSubmersiblePedestal
Motor locationSealed inside pit, underwaterOn a shaft above the pit
NoiseQuiet (water muffles motor)Louder (motor exposed to air)
Lifespan5-15 years15-25 years
Cost$100-$400$60-$200
Horsepower1/3 to 1 HP1/3 to 1/2 HP
Handles solidsYes (screened intake)Less effectively
Pit size neededStandard 18"+ diameterCan work in smaller pits
MaintenanceMust remove from pit to serviceEasy access above pit
Finished basementBetter (hidden, quiet)Visible shaft above pit

Our Recommendation for Chicago

Submersible is the better choice for most Chicago homes. It's quieter, more powerful, handles debris better, and is completely hidden in the pit — important for finished basements. The shorter lifespan is offset by the superior performance during Chicago's heavy storms.

title: Point-of-Use vs Whole-House Water Filter: Which Is Right?
author: Plumbers 911 Chicago
content:
Water filtration systems come in two main categories: point-of-use (POU) filters that treat water at a single faucet, and whole-house (POE) systems that treat all water entering your home.

Comparison

FactorPoint-of-Use (Under-Sink)Whole-House
TreatsOne faucet (drinking/cooking water)All water in the home
Cost$150-$500 installed$1,000-$3,000+ installed
Filter changesEvery 6-12 months ($20-$60)Every 3-12 months ($50-$200)
Removes chlorineYesYes
Removes leadYes (if NSF 53 certified)Some models (check specs)
Protects appliancesNoYes (removes sediment, chlorine)
Better shower waterNoYes (less chlorine, softer skin/hair)

Which Should You Choose?

  • Just want better drinking water? Point-of-use under-sink filter is the cost-effective choice
  • Concerned about lead? Under-sink reverse osmosis system (best lead removal)
  • Want filtered water everywhere? Whole-house carbon filter
  • Maximum protection? Whole-house filter + under-sink RO for drinking water

Call 833-758-6911 for water filter installation.

title: Electric vs Gas Water Heater: Complete Comparison for Chicago
author: Plumbers 911 Chicago
content:
Most Chicago homes have access to natural gas (Peoples Gas or Nicor Gas), giving you the choice between electric and gas water heaters. Here's how to decide.

Comparison

FactorGasElectric
Purchase cost$400-$1,000$300-$800
Installation costHigher (gas line, venting)Lower (electrical connection)
Monthly operating cost$30-$50 (cheaper in Chicago)$40-$80
Recovery rate (50 gal)~40 gallons/hour~20 gallons/hour
Lifespan8-12 years10-15 years
Efficiency60-70% standard, 90%+ high-eff95-100% (nearly all energy heats water)
Works during power outageYes (if pilot/standing pilot)No
Venting requiredYes (exhaust gases)No
SafetyCO risk if improperly ventedNo combustion risks

For Chicago Homes

Gas is usually the better choice because natural gas is significantly cheaper than electricity in the Chicago area. A gas water heater also recovers faster, which matters for larger families. However, electric is better if you don't have a gas line to the water heater location, or if you're planning to go all-electric with solar panels.
